review_text: If you are a superhero action dunderhead with the IQ of a banana, better off sticking with Marvel comic films. If you love thinking and challenging your brain, this is an extraordinary film that sets the sci-fi standard and still holds up many decades later.

As a 14 year old watching this for the first when it was released in 1968 I was left totally unsure of the what the film was saying. Today after watching a re-screening at IMAX (didn’t have this in 1968!) I was left pondering just how futuristic it was further time. To think visual phone calls were unimaginable back then, and that PanAm could have been envisaged as a space travel provider (oops), but IBM would still be around as they are even today. But alas 2001 came and went and we haven’t been permanently on the moon or travelled beyond as humans. All that aside, the film remains an all-time classic and trail blazer for what has followed in the sci-fi genre. Have always admired the decision to use so much classical music. The special effects were outstanding for the time and the creepy HAL as an AI fiend may still prove to be a promise of things to come.

Many people criticize this film for being too long or for having scenic shots and not much dialog, especially in comparison with the usual fast-paced movie of today, but this is an undeniable classic, not only for its era but currently as well. It's fine to like faster-paced films, but disliking or talking poorly of this movie for being slow is baffling.

Only film l have ever seen three times and it holds up well after 50 years. Reading the reviews its obvious that most do not get the plot so people who like it stress its visual quality and special effects while those dislike it are just bored. You have to read Arthur Clarke’s The Sentinel to get what it is about. There are two things in particular to say about it. First it makes more demands on the audience than any major release because for a lot of the time you don’t really know whats going on. Second it is the most ambitious film ever made as it purports to tell the story of our species. There are many other things l could write but for me, there is 2001 and all other films.
Just watched this. Hard to believe it's from 1968. Budget of 10 million and currently at 145 million in box office take. 57 years old and as relevant and intriguing today as then.
TOO SLOW!! I COULD BARELY STAY AWAKE. I STAYED TO THE END BECAUSE THE REVIEWS WERE SO HIGH. ?

A truly cinematic masterpiece! If you appreciate film and cinematography, it's something you will enjoy. Drop your expectations. Step outside of today's depth-lacking, wash/rinse/repeat movies to see the film for the work of art that it is, and was, for today and at the time it was written. Yes, you will be thinking "what the f*** did i just watch", but that's the point. It will leave you searching for answers on reddit threads as you lie in bed "what was the meaning of ?" Grab a copy of the book, which was written after the movie (not the other way around), to fill in gaps and explain more of the films meanings and symbolism.

2001: Uma Odisseia no Espaço permanece uma das obras mais cientificamente sofisticadas já produzidas, uma síntese rara entre rigor físico, especulação cosmológica e estética minimalista. Kubrick e Arthur C. Clarke constroem uma narrativa que explora desde evolução biológica até inteligência artificial e hipóteses avançadas de vida pós-humana, sempre com um respeito incomum pela plausibilidade científica. A representação da mecânica orbital e dos deslocamentos espaciais é extraordinariamente precisa: movimentos sem ruído, ausência de fricção, e trajetórias que obedecem a dinâmicas newtonianas reais, calculadas em função de vetores de aceleração e órbitas keplerianas. A rotação da estação espacial e da nave Discovery para gerar gravidade artificial se baseia em princípios corretos de força centrífuga e aceleração rotacional, conceito raramente compreendido tão bem em ficções da época. O Monolito, embora deliberadamente enigmático, dialoga com proposições genuínas da astrobiologia e da física teórica, evocando noções de engenharia extraterrestre de escala pós-humana, inteligência não-carbono e possíveis mecanismos de manipulação de informação molecular. Sua função como catalisador evolutivo se apoia em debates modernos sobre bioevolução dirigida e saltos adaptativos que fogem ao puro gradualismo darwiniano. A sequência de Júpiter — frequentemente descrita como "psicodélica" — pode ser interpretada como uma visualização especulativa de uma topologia extradimensional, compatível com teorias contemporâneas de wormholes navegáveis, espaço-tempo não trivial e superfícies de contorno fora da métrica comum. A travessia final sugere a existência de um ambiente obedecendo a regras de geometria diferencial alterada, algo discutido em modelos de gravidade quântica e universos em hipervolumes n-dimensionais. HAL 9000, por sua vez, permanece uma das representações mais tecnicamente sólidas de IA já feitas: um sistema baseado em processamento heurístico, inferência lógica, planejamento autônomo e resolução contraditória de diretrizes, antecipando debates modernos sobre alinhamento de IA, falhas de prioridade e paradoxos computacionais de obediência irrestrita. A obra, no conjunto, transcende ficção científica: é uma exploração audiovisual sobre como física, tecnologia e inteligência convergem para pontos de ruptura evolucionária. 2001 não oferece respostas — propõe hipóteses. E faz isso com uma precisão científica e técnica que, até hoje, poucos filmes ousaram alcançar.

It's a good idea but not well portrayed, music is unengaging and feels boring, there is a 10 minute scene where it's just breathing and air hissing. I had to force myself to watch this, would recommend the book instead. Terrible

Kubrick saw the future. Kubrick revolutionized science fiction cinema forever with '2001: A Space Odyssey', a work that, since the late sixties, remains a creative and conceptual milestone impossible to forget or erase from people's memory. A legacy that transcends its time, not only due to its technical audacity but also because of the philosophical depth with which it addresses the relationship between humanity, technology, and space. One of the aspects I pay the most attention to is its sound design. Kubrick understood that the representation of sound had to be faithful to its nature: silence. It was a risky decision to forgo sound effects in pursuit of a striking realism. That silence does not diminish impact; on the contrary, it intensifies it to the limit. A contrast effect, enhancing the immersion and solitude of the vastness of space. It is a choice that sets the film apart from other major science fiction productions and reaffirms its identity as a contemplative work. The cinematography is simply breathtaking. Kubrick and Geoffrey Unsworth create images that, even decades later, remain hypnotic: floating spacecraft with elegance, shots that perfectly convey human smallness against the universe, and compositions that function as moving paintings. All of this goes hand in hand with a soundtrack based on classical pieces, giving the whole a powerful emotional dimension. The first part constitutes one of the most emblematic sequences. An intertwining of primitive humanity and space exploration in the same narrative thread. Equally thrilling for the general viewer as for any cinephile. These two timeless moments are connected by a temporal ellipsis through a match cut. Everyone talks about this ellipsis, and rightly so; it is the greatest narrative ellipsis in cinema. Summarizing millions of years in less than a second. Although the match cutting technique is not technically perfect, Kubrick clearly prioritized narrative over technicalities. In fact, it is said that Kubrick intentionally sought that "error" in the editing so that the cut would be as abrupt as possible. Although other directors had already opened the door to AI in cinema, it was Kubrick who brought it to the center of the cultural debate. The film anticipates issues that remain relevant today: the autonomy of machines, the possible confrontation between humans and intelligent systems, and the ethical implications of technological development. The dialogues pose constant questions that we still ask ourselves today, turning the work into a warning ahead of its time. The final part is full of symbolism and remains a sequence that is thoroughly discussed and analyzed. With a personal response from the director to evolution, human destiny, and transcendence. An enigma, deeply sensory, inviting multiple interpretations without sacrificing its emotional delivery. An absolute masterpiece. Innovative, daring, and poetic. A redefinition of the genre. An influential legacy for filmmakers, philosophers, and scientists. A film that is not just watched but lived and experienced. A monumental reflection (no pun intended) on who we are, where we are going, and what it truly means to evolve.

2001: A Space Odyssey follows humanity’s evolution from the dawn of man to a future defined by artificial intelligence, deep space exploration, and a mysterious alien monolith guiding our next steps. The story moves through eras, from prehistoric discovery to a mission to Jupiter, where HAL 9000, an AI meant to be flawless, slowly unravels and creates one of the most iconic suspense arcs in film history. This is a movie that was far ahead of its time. The visual effects look like it was shot in the last ten years, not in the 1960s, and the scale of the filmmaking still feels unmatched today. Kubrick delivers a slow burn, atmospheric suspense that keeps you captivated throughout, turning the film into a true sci fi mind bender. It is rightfully considered one of the best movies ever made, ambitious, beautiful, unsettling, and unforgettable. 99/100
Nope nope nope. I'm really sorry. But cannot recommend.

I was hyped for this movie for a while, but it was kinda a disappointment. Besides the dragged out pacing, the story wasnt there much. While Hal 9000 is a pop culture staple villain, his movie counterpart barely had a pretense due to the movies dragged out pacing. The beginning sequence was unnecessarily long as well. It shouldve been around 5 minutes instead of around 20. The parts where people talked were good, however perhaps there werent enough of them. The special effects were good, but they dont make up for the storys unusual nature. I mean no disrespect to Kubrick or any of the people who worked on this film, its just that this film isnt for me
I’ll never be able to explain why I love this movie so much, but I fully understand why others might hate it.
It’s very pretty but that’s about it, wrap it up boys

I can’t find a flaw in this film. It will leave you wondering how it’s possible Kubrick shot many of the scenes. For this reason alone it should not stop you from watching. It simply doesn’t feel like a film from 1969. The scale of it all will from early mankind to the space age will leave you in awe. This film is widely celebrated by many, and I strongly believe it is justified and in no way overrated. It did justice to the book written by Arthur C Clarke in my opinion. Touches on space travel, human evolution, and artificial intelligence. Possibly one of the most influential films in history, and this is by no means an overstatement in my opinion. For those who are not fans of science fiction this film will likely change that.

A Timeless Masterpiece of Sight, Sound, and Silence. 2001: A Space Odyssey remains not just a science fiction film, but a cinematic Everest — a work of unparalleled ambition, intellect, and aesthetic precision. Over half a century later, Stanley Kubrick’s visuals still challenge contemporary CGI, with practical effects, meticulous model work, and pioneering cinematography creating a sense of scale, realism, and weight that feels both timeless and otherworldly. What truly sets the film apart are its long, dialogue-free sequences, which transform silence into suspense, contemplation, and awe. Every movement of the spacecraft, every flicker of HAL 9000, every carefully choreographed frame is imbued with intention, making the viewer acutely aware of isolation, tension, and cosmic mystery. Coupled with the ethereal, classical score — Strauss, Ligeti, and others — the film achieves a hypnotic rhythm, blending human grandeur and existential dread in perfect harmony. 2001 is as much an intellectual journey as a visual one, exploring evolution, technology, and humanity’s place in the universe. Its audacity, rigor, and artistry remain unmatched, and in every sense, it is the greatest science fiction film ever made — a work that continues to inspire, challenge, and mesmerize.

I waited about 30 years to watch this movie... Now that i finally have, my opinion is not a popular one. Where do i start? The visuals and cinematography are amazing. It's incredible how most movies that come out today (around 57 years later) look like amateur college student movies, compared to this one. On that regard, it is one of the best movies/best looking movies of all time. Credit where credit is due. The pace and repetitiveness ... For people who do not like the movie, this is probably the target of most criticism. I get that at the time the movie came out, watching a simple/mundane thing that could be a few seconds or 1 minute, being stretched for many minutes, was probably cool and gave the audience the sense of being in space. It was also a showboat thing, like: "Look at how realistic and gorgeous our special effects are. Let's stay in this simple scene for 10 minutes.". But i truly believe that even for that era's standards, it was still painfully slow. You can see this just by looking at movies that came right after. I also get that the pace is slow on purpose, to induce reflection, isolation, and a sense of realism on how things would go in space/space travelling. But to me, that sounds more like an excuse made by the director, in order to justify the torture he had the audience go through, for his own selfish, eccentric and self-indulgent reasons. The constant annoying, drawn out, repetitive sounds make it even worse. Sound is important to give a movie more weight and deliver the message; but you don't have to hammer the audience with it. Just use it for a few seconds or a minute and we will get it. Don't torture us. And i say this, as someone who, contrary to most people i know, enjoys a movie with a pace that forces you to slow down and allows you to exist in that world for a few hours. In essence, this movie proves that sometimes, what can make an artist a genius, can also turn him/her into an unberable talent to deal with, because all humans, regardless of their talent, have egos that can go unchecked. And although advocating for balance in a creative endeavour can sound counter intuitive, here, i think it's justified. But that might be my own ego talking. I also heard people say that this movie is supposed to be watched on lsd, mushrooms, and all kinds of drugs. To me, that is one of the most ridiculous things i have heard or one of the biggest excuses to defend a piece of art or its creator. No movie should be intended to watch under the effect of drugs. The idea of the director and writer having had those experiences with drugs did occur to me tho, as some of the scenes were a bit trippy to say the least. The characters... No interesting characters. No character arcs. Nothing... The more impactful one, Hal 9000, is also not interesting; more like, misterious, due to what his true motives are. The plot... In the first 30-60 min, it was predictable, yet misterious, and it had me hooked. Then, it became very predictable. I can see it not being that when the movie came out, but in todays age, with all the amazing sci-fi stories in movies, videogames and animes out there, not only it's predictable, it's almost non existent. Yes, i understand that most of the story is told through imagery; i understand you have to let yourself go deeper and beyond what you're seeing to truly understand it. But it's still not enough for a movie of this kind. And again, i say this as someone who doesn't mind slow movies; movies told mostly through images. Lovers of this movie will often make the point that young, modern people, won't appreciate it and understand it. That you have to truly dive deep down to see the big picture. That you have to be on drugs to understand it. Well, i am not young; i have always enjoyed slow movies. I always try to see the bigger picture. I don't mind more artsy, deeper meaning stories/movies that don't babysit the audience, etc I don't do drugs tho. So all of that sounds not only like an offence to my movie viewer intelligence; sounds like a snobby defence from lovers of this movie; and it sounds like another excuse. An excuse for not knowing what to write or for lazyness. In the end, i thought i was going to watch something special that was going to blow my mind, but i was disappointed. I understood the idea behind it. I understood the final message, but i needed more to feel satisfied and justify being tortured for almost 3 hours. But i have to give it props on many things. Two of them, are the way it accurately depicts the government's lies to the people they rule over, so they can further their true goals. I feel like that is so true, now more than ever. And the other, is the influence it had on so many other movies. Some of the greatest movies ever made clearly took inspiration from this one. For that, i am thankful this one exists, because it created the frame for those other ones to explore some of its themes and ideas in a much better way. And there lies the true genius of this movie. In a weird way, it brings one its main themes to life. Which one i'm talking about? You will have to find out for yourself. But i will say this: it's such a bad movie, that it's a good movie; it's such a good movie, that it's a bad movie.

What a long, strange trip it's been. What does it all mean? Is that really what matters? A visual treat, an aural delight, the pleasures to be found in colour and geometry, the beauty of silence, the wonder of creativity itself. A film that defies precise analysis but gives the viewer the gift of free interpretation and letting the imagination run riot, is freed of earthly confines.

2001: A Space Odyssey, I'm quite in awe right now. I'll honestly say that this is one of the best film I've ever watched in my entire life. Oh gosh, the cinematography, the artistry, creativeness, the rawness, the idea of the universe and beyond the infinite, the visual effects, the impact, and the beauty of this film is out of this world! Mind you it was released in 1968 which makes me yell to someone right now. Mr. Stanley Kubrick was ahead of his time. His artistry and creativeness in this film was so breathtakingly amazing! With the non-verbal scene and majestic explosion of visual effects, it tells us to look for the beauty of the things around us or the universe to be exact. This is one of the missing and important aspect in the film in which most of the films released today were lacking. The beauty inside the film that makes you want to crave more and think how beautiful our world could be; not just a little of this or that and called it a "masterpiece". The hint of betrayal, disappointment, the feeling of being alone in a wide space, the fear, the detachment and a more of bit of Science was the best ever scene for me. It blew my mind especially when they're hinting us what might happen in the future with the help of computer and what we might explore more about our universe.

A benchmark in hard science-fiction cinema, Kubrick’s masterpiece dazzles the senses as well as proposes questions that few films of its genre hardly ever asks (or answers). It is a precursor to films like Blade Runner, Star Wars, & Interstellar, respectively. This is a must watch.

I could see how the effects would’ve been phenomenal when it debuted - but this movie lacked almost any plot whatsoever and was horribly long and boring. This entire movie could have been 20m long and we would’ve all walked away with the same intended feels, themes, and detailed appreciation for a futuristic set and effects. I skipped through many of the unnecessary long sequences and I still feel I gave it too much of my time for the entertainment that it could not provide.

Gonna be honest, I went into this movie with pretty decent expectations, but I had no clue what to expect. Like I heard it was good, but that's it. And I can say it was very well done. The first bit was so different than anything I've ever seen, but I enjoyed the picture it was painting. The second half was very good, but I was honestly confused a little. I enjoy the movies that leave things up to the audience to interpret, but I feel like they didn't give us enough to brainstorm. Gonna give this another watch, and I'm sure I'll enjoy it more. Also, for 1968, I thought the look of the film was extraordinary. The acting was fantastic and the visuals were great.
Good but could've been like 1 hour shorter.

I saw this in theaters couple months ago. Little dialogue in this film. The visuals are insane, it still holds up even though it was made in 1968. If you love visuals this movie is for you.
